task_description: In this task, you will create a program that works with a basic array to understand how data is stored sequentially in memory. This task is important because arrays are the foundation of almost all data structures used in real-world software systems. Step by step, you must declare an array, insert multiple elements into it, display all elements using a loop, and retrieve a specific element using an index provided by the user. You should also handle invalid index access to avoid runtime errors. Hint: Use loops for traversal and always check that the index is within valid bounds before accessing the array.
expected_output: The program correctly stores elements, displays them in order, and retrieves values using a valid index
evaluation_criteria: Correct use of arrays, proper traversal logic, safe index handling, clean and readable output

task_description: This task requires you to write a program that reverses the elements of an array without using any built-in reverse functions. Reversing arrays helps you understand element swapping, indexing, and iteration, which are core skills for data manipulation tasks in internships. Step by step, you should use two pointers starting from the beginning and end of the array, swap elements, and move inward until the array is fully reversed. Hint: Stop swapping when the start index becomes greater than or equal to the end index.
expected_output: Array elements are displayed in complete reverse order
evaluation_criteria: Correct swapping logic, no built-in reverse usage, accurate index management

task_title: Find Maximum and Minimum Values in an Array
task_description: In this task, you will develop a program to find the maximum and minimum values stored in an array. This operation is frequently used in analytics, reporting, and monitoring systems in real-world applications. Step by step, traverse the array from the first element, compare each value, and update the maximum and minimum variables accordingly. Hint: Initialize both maximum and minimum with the first element of the array before traversal.
expected_output: Correct maximum and minimum values are displayed
evaluation_criteria: Accurate comparison logic, full traversal, correct initialization

task_title: Count Even and Odd Numbers in an Array
task_description: This task focuses on categorizing data by counting even and odd numbers in an array. Such operations are common in data filtering and validation tasks during internships. Step by step, loop through the array, check each element using the modulus operator, and maintain separate counters for even and odd values. Hint: Use value % 2 to determine whether a number is even or odd.
expected_output: Correct count of even and odd numbers displayed
evaluation_criteria: Accurate conditional logic, correct counting, complete traversal

task_description: In this task, you will implement the linear search algorithm to find a specific value in an array. Linear search is one of the simplest searching techniques and helps build problem-solving foundations. Step by step, compare each array element with the target value until it is found or the array ends. Hint: Exit the loop early when the element is found to improve efficiency.
expected_output: Program correctly reports whether the element exists
evaluation_criteria: Correct search logic, proper loop control, correct result reporting

task_title: Insert Element at a Specific Index in Array
task_description: This task requires inserting a new element at a specific index within an array. This is important for understanding how insertion affects existing data and memory layout. Step by step, shift elements to the right starting from the end of the array, then place the new element at the given index. Hint: Always shift elements backward to avoid overwriting data.
expected_output: Element inserted at the correct position in the array
evaluation_criteria: Correct shifting logic, no data loss, valid index handling

task_title: Delete Element from Array Using Index
task_description: In this task, you will delete an element from an array using a given index. Deletion operations are common when managing records in applications. Step by step, validate the index, shift elements to the left to fill the gap, and reduce the logical size of the array. Hint: Handle deletion of the first and last elements carefully.
evaluation_criteria: Proper index validation, correct shifting logic, stable output

task_description: This task focuses on understanding the stack data structure and its Last-In-First-Out behavior. Stacks are widely used in function calls, undo/redo systems, and expression evaluation. Step by step, explain stack operations such as push and pop using real-world examples. Hint: Imagine stacking plates one on top of another.
expected_output: Clear written explanation of stack behavior and use cases
evaluation_criteria: Concept accuracy, clear explanation, relevant real-world examples

task_description: In this task, you will implement a stack using an array. This practical implementation helps convert theory into real coding skills expected from interns. Step by step, define a stack array, maintain a top variable, and implement push and pop operations with boundary checks. Hint: Top should start at -1 for an empty stack.
expected_output: Working stack with correct push and pop operations
evaluation_criteria: Correct LIFO behavior, boundary condition handling

task_description: This task focuses specifically on the push operation in a stack. Push is used to add elements to the top of the stack. Step by step, increment the top pointer and insert the new value into the array. Hint: Always check for stack overflow before pushing.

In this task, you will implement the pop operation to remove the top element from the stack. Step by step, check whether the stack is empty, retrieve the top element, and decrement the top pointer. Hint: Never pop from an empty stack.

This task introduces the queue data structure and its First-In-First-Out behavior. Queues are commonly used in scheduling systems, request handling, and buffering. Step by step, explain enqueue and dequeue operations with real-world examples. Hint: Think of people standing in a line.

In this task, you will implement a queue using an array. Step by step, define front and rear pointers and implement enqueue and dequeue operations. This task is important because queues are frequently used in backend and system-level software. Hint: Initialize front and rear properly before operations.

This task focuses on adding elements to a queue using enqueue operation. Step by step, insert an element at the rear position and update the rear pointer. Hint: Always check for queue overflow before enqueue.
In this task, you will remove elements from the front of a queue. Step by step, retrieve the front element and update the front pointer. Hint: Check for underflow before dequeue.

This task introduces the concept of circular queues, which improve space utilization in array-based queues. Step by step, explain how the rear wraps around to the beginning using modulo arithmetic. Hint: Use circular indexing logic.

In this task, you will implement a basic circular queue using an array. Step by step, apply modulo arithmetic to manage front and rear movement efficiently. Hint: Use (index + 1) % size to move circularly.
evaluation_criteria: Correct circular behavior, proper pointer updates

task_description: This task focuses on understanding singly linked lists and why they are used instead of arrays in some cases. Step by step, explain nodes, pointers, and dynamic memory usage. Hint: Each node points to the next node.
expected_output: Clear explanation of singly linked list

task_description: In this task, you will create the basic node structure for a singly linked list. Step by step, define data and a pointer to the next node. Hint: Keep node structure minimal and clear.

This task requires traversing a singly linked list and displaying all node values. Step by step, start from the head and move through each next pointer until null is reached. Hint: Use a temporary pointer for traversal.
expected_output: All linked list elements displayed correctly
evaluation_criteria: Correct traversal logic, no infinite loops

task_title: Insert Node at Beginning of Singly Linked List
task_description: In this task, you will insert a new node at the beginning of a linked list. Step by step, point the new node to the current head and update the head pointer. Hint: New node becomes the new head.

task_title: Insert Node at End of Singly Linked List
task_description: This task focuses on inserting a node at the end of a linked list. Step by step, traverse to the last node and update its next pointer. Hint: Last node’s next should point to the new node.

task_title: Delete Node from Singly Linked List
task_description: In this task, you will delete a node from a singly linked list. Step by step, locate the node, update pointers, and free memory if required. Hint: Handle deletion of the head node separately.
evaluation_criteria: Correct deletion logic, safe pointer updates

task_description: This task introduces doubly linked lists and their advantages over singly linked lists. Step by step, explain how nodes link in both directions. Hint: Nodes contain previous and next pointers.
expected_output: Clear explanation of doubly linked list

task_description: In this task, you will create a node structure for a doubly linked list. Step by step, include data, previous pointer, and next pointer. Hint: Both pointers are required.
expected_output: Correct doubly linked list node created

task_description: This task focuses on understanding time complexity and why it matters in software performance. Step by step, explain Big-O notation with simple examples. Hint: Focus on how execution time grows.

task_title: Analyze Time Complexity of Simple Loops
task_description: In this task, you will analyze the time complexity of simple loop-based programs. Step by step, count the number of iterations and determine the Big-O notation. Hint: A single loop usually results in O(n).
This task introduces space complexity and memory usage analysis. Step by step, explain how extra variables and data structures affect memory. Hint: Consider auxiliary space.

In this task, you will compare arrays and linked lists in terms of memory usage, access speed, and flexibility. Step by step, list advantages and disadvantages of each. Hint: Think static vs dynamic memory.
This task introduces recursion and its importance in data structures. Step by step, explain base case and recursive case using simple examples. Hint: A function calls itself.

In this task, you will implement a simple recursive function such as factorial or sum of numbers. Step by step, define base case and recursive call properly. Hint: Base case prevents infinite recursion.
This task explains how recursion uses the call stack internally. Step by step, describe how function calls are pushed and popped. Hint: Each call waits for return.

task_title: Identify Real-World Applications of Data Structures
task_description: In this task, you will identify real-world applications of data structures such as stacks, queues, and linked lists. Step by step, map structures to real systems. Hint: Use OS, browsers, or apps.
This task focuses on understanding sorting and why it is important in data processing. Step by step, explain ordering of data. Hint: Sorting improves search efficiency.
In this task, you will implement the bubble sort algorithm. Step by step, compare adjacent elements and swap them if needed. Hint: Largest element bubbles to the end.
This task introduces searching concepts and their importance. Step by step, explain how searching locates data. Hint: Searching finds element position.
This task requires implementing linear search on an array. Step by step, check each element sequentially. Hint: Stop when found.

task_title: Understand Role of Data Structures in Software Systems
task_description: This task focuses on explaining how data structures support software systems such as databases and applications. Step by step, connect performance and structure. Hint: Efficiency matters.

task_title: Write Clean and Readable Code for Data Structures
task_description: In this task, you will refactor existing data structure code to improve readability. Step by step, rename variables, format code, and add comments. Hint: Clean code is easier to maintain.

task_title: Test and Review All Easy-Level Implementations
task_description: In this final task, you will test all easy-level data structure programs and fix any errors. Step by step, run each program and verify outputs. Hint: Debug carefully.

In this task, you will design and implement a complete stack data structure using an array. This task is important because stacks are widely used in real-world systems such as undo/redo functionality, function call handling, and expression evaluation. Step by step, you must define an array, maintain a top pointer, and implement core stack operations including push, pop, and peek. You should also handle stack overflow and underflow conditions gracefully with proper messages. Hint: Initialize the top pointer to -1 and always validate boundaries before operations.
expected_output: Stack supports push, pop, and peek operations correctly
evaluation_criteria: orrect LIFO behavior, proper boundary handling, clean and logical implementation

task_description: In this task, you will create a program that checks whether an expression contains balanced parentheses using a stack. This problem demonstrates a real-world use of stacks in syntax checking and compilers. Step by step, scan the expression character by character, push opening brackets onto the stack, and pop when matching closing brackets are encountered. At the end, ensure the stack is empty. Hint: Handle different types of brackets such as (), {}, and [].
expected_output: Program correctly identifies balanced and unbalanced expressions
evaluation_criteria: Correct stack usage, accurate bracket matching, edge case handling

task_description: This task requires reversing a string using a stack data structure instead of built-in functions. This task is important because it strengthens understanding of stack behavior and data reversal logic. Step by step, push each character of the string onto the stack and then pop them to form the reversed string. Hint: The LIFO nature of stacks naturally reverses order.
evaluation_criteria: Correct stack operations, no built-in reverse usage

task_description: In this task, you will implement a queue using a linked list instead of an array. This is important because linked lists allow dynamic memory allocation and efficient enqueue/dequeue operations. Step by step, maintain front and rear pointers, implement enqueue at rear and dequeue from front, and handle empty queue cases. Hint: When the queue becomes empty, update both front and rear to null.
expected_output: Queue operations work correctly using linked list
evaluation_criteria: Correct FIFO behavior, pointer management, no memory issues

task_description: This task focuses on implementing a circular queue using an array to efficiently utilize memory. Circular queues are used in buffering systems and scheduling algorithms. Step by step, apply modulo arithmetic to manage front and rear pointers and handle full and empty conditions. Hint: Queue is full when (rear + 1) % size == front.
evaluation_criteria: Correct circular logic, proper overflow/underflow handling

task_description: In this task, you will implement a deque that allows insertion and deletion from both front and rear ends. Deques are used in task scheduling and sliding window problems. Step by step, design operations to insert and delete from both ends while maintaining correctness. Hint: Carefully manage front and rear indices.
evaluation_criteria: Correct logic for both ends, stable behavior

task_title: Implement Singly Linked List with Full Operations
task_description: This task requires building a complete singly linked list implementation. This includes insertion at beginning, insertion at end, deletion by value, and traversal. Linked lists are fundamental in memory-efficient data management. Step by step, handle pointer updates carefully for each operation. Hint: Always check for empty list before operations.
evaluation_criteria: Correct pointer handling, no memory leaks

task_title: Search an Element in Singly Linked List
task_description: In this task, you will search for a given element in a singly linked list. Searching linked lists is common in record-based systems. Step by step, traverse the list node by node and compare values until found or end is reached. Hint: Use a temporary pointer for traversal.

This task focuses on finding the length of a linked list. Knowing list size is important for many algorithms and validations. Step by step, traverse the list and increment a counter until the end. Hint: Start counting from the head node.
expected_output: Correct length of linked list displayed

task_description: In this task, you will reverse a singly linked list iteratively. Reversing linked lists is a common interview and internship task. Step by step, update next pointers carefully using previous, current, and next references. Hint: Maintain three pointers during reversal.
evaluation_criteria: Correct pointer updates, no data loss

task_title: Implement Doubly Linked List with Operations
task_description: This task requires implementing a doubly linked list with insertion, deletion, and traversal in both directions. Doubly linked lists allow bidirectional navigation in applications like browsers. Step by step, manage both next and previous pointers. Hint: Update both links during insertion and deletion.

task_title: Find Middle Element of Linked List
task_description: In this task, you will find the middle element of a linked list. This task introduces efficient traversal techniques. Step by step, use slow and fast pointers to locate the middle in one pass. Hint: Fast pointer moves twice as fast as slow pointer.

This task focuses on detecting whether a linked list contains a loop. Loop detection is critical in avoiding infinite traversals. Step by step, use Floyd’s cycle detection algorithm with two pointers. Hint: If slow and fast pointers meet, a loop exists.

In this task, you will merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list. This operation is common in data processing pipelines. Step by step, compare node values and rearrange pointers accordingly. Hint: Use a dummy node to simplify logic.

This task requires implementing a stack using a linked list instead of an array. This approach avoids fixed size limitations. Step by step, perform push and pop operations at the head of the list. Hint: Head represents top of stack.
evaluation_criteria: Correct LIFO behavior, dynamic memory handling

task_description: In this task, you will implement a queue using two stacks. This task improves algorithmic thinking and understanding of data structure transformation. Step by step, use one stack for enqueue and another for dequeue. Hint: Transfer elements only when needed.

This task focuses on converting infix expressions to postfix using a stack. This is important in expression evaluation systems. Step by step, handle operator precedence and parentheses. Hint: Use a stack for operators.

In this task, you will evaluate a postfix expression using a stack. Expression evaluation is common in compilers and calculators. Step by step, push operands and apply operators. Hint: Pop operands in correct order.

task_title: Implement Binary Search on Sorted Array
task_description: This task introduces binary search, an efficient searching algorithm used in large datasets. Step by step, divide the search space in half based on comparisons. Hint: Array must be sorted.
expected_output: Correct search result using binary search

task_title: Compare Linear Search and Binary Search
task_description: In this task, you will compare linear and binary search in terms of time complexity and use cases. Step by step, analyze best, worst, and average cases. Hint: Binary search requires sorted data.

This task focuses on implementing selection sort. Although not efficient for large data, it helps understand selection logic. Step by step, select minimum element and swap. Hint: Reduce unsorted portion gradually.
In this task, you will implement insertion sort, commonly used for small datasets. Step by step, insert each element into its correct position. Hint: Shift elements to make space.

task_title: Analyze Time Complexity of Sorting Algorithms
task_description: This task requires analyzing time complexity of bubble, selection, and insertion sort. Step by step, compare their performance. Hint: Focus on worst-case complexity.
In this task, you will generate Fibonacci numbers using recursion. This task reinforces recursive thinking. Step by step, define base cases and recursive calls. Hint: Fibonacci grows exponentially.
This task improves recursive Fibonacci using memoization. This introduces dynamic programming concepts. Step by step, store previously computed values. Hint: Use an array or map.

This task introduces tree data structures and hierarchical data representation. Trees are used in file systems and databases. Step by step, explain root, child, and leaf nodes. Hint: Trees are non-linear structures.
In this task, you will implement a node structure for a binary tree. Step by step, define left and right child pointers. Hint: Node contains data and two references.
This task focuses on implementing inorder, preorder, and postorder traversals. Traversals are essential for processing tree data. Step by step, use recursion. Hint: Follow correct traversal order.

In this task, you will calculate the height of a binary tree. Height calculation is used in balancing and optimization. Step by step, compute height recursively. Hint: Height is max of left and right subtree plus one.

task_title: Count Leaf Nodes in Binary Tree
task_description: This task requires counting leaf nodes in a binary tree. Leaf nodes represent end points. Step by step, traverse tree and count nodes with no children. Hint: Leaf has no left and right.
In this task, you will implement insertion in a Binary Search Tree. BSTs are used for efficient searching. Step by step, compare values and insert accordingly. Hint: Left < root < right.

task_title: Search Element in Binary Search Tree
task_description: This task focuses on searching an element in a BST. Step by step, traverse left or right based on comparison. Hint: Use BST ordering.

task_title: Find Minimum and Maximum in BST
task_description: In this task, you will find minimum and maximum values in a BST. Step by step, move leftmost for min and rightmost for max. Hint: No need to traverse whole tree.

task_title: Implement Level Order Traversal of Tree
task_description: This task introduces level order traversal using a queue. Step by step, visit nodes level by level. Hint: Use queue for BFS.
This task introduces heaps and their properties. Heaps are used in priority queues. Step by step, explain max-heap and min-heap. Hint: Parent-child relationship matters.
In this task, you will implement insertion in a max heap. Step by step, insert element and heapify upward. Hint: Compare with parent.
This task focuses on min heap insertion. Step by step, ensure smallest element is at root. Hint: Heapify up.
In this task, you will delete the root element from a heap. Step by step, replace root and heapify down. Hint: Restore heap property.

This task requires implementing a priority queue using a heap. Priority queues are used in scheduling systems. Step by step, enqueue with priority and dequeue highest priority. Hint: Heap fits priority queues naturally.
This task introduces graphs and their real-world applications. Graphs represent networks. Step by step, explain vertices and edges. Hint: Think social networks.
In this task, you will represent a graph using an adjacency matrix. Step by step, create matrix and fill edges. Hint: Matrix size is VxV.
This task focuses on representing a graph using adjacency lists. Step by step, store neighbors for each vertex. Hint: More space efficient than matrix.
In this task, you will implement BFS traversal of a graph. BFS is used in shortest path algorithms. Step by step, use a queue and visited array. Hint: Visit neighbors level by level.
This task requires implementing DFS traversal of a graph. DFS is used in cycle detection. Step by step, use recursion or stack. Hint: Go deep before backtracking.
In this task, you will compare BFS and DFS in terms of traversal order, use cases, and complexity. Step by step, analyze differences. Hint: BFS uses queue, DFS uses stack.

task_title: Implement Binary Search Tree with Core Operations
task_description: In this task, you will design and implement a complete Binary Search Tree (BST) that supports insertion, searching, and inorder traversal operations. This task is important because BSTs are widely used in databases, indexing systems, and memory-efficient searching applications. Step by step, you must define a tree node structure, implement insertion logic that maintains BST properties, implement search functionality, and perform inorder traversal to display sorted elements. Hint: Ensure that for every node, values in the left subtree are smaller and values in the right subtree are larger.
expected_output: BST correctly inserts nodes, searches values, and displays sorted traversal
evaluation_criteria: Correct BST property maintenance, accurate traversal output, efficient search logic

task_title: Delete Node from Binary Search Tree
task_description: This task focuses on implementing node deletion in a Binary Search Tree, which is more complex than insertion. This operation is important because real systems require removal of records while maintaining structure. Step by step, handle deletion cases including leaf node, node with one child, and node with two children. Hint: For nodes with two children, replace with inorder successor or predecessor.
expected_output: Node deleted correctly while BST properties remain intact
evaluation_criteria: Correct handling of all deletion cases, tree integrity

task_description: In this task, you will verify whether a given binary tree satisfies Binary Search Tree properties. Validation is important in systems where tree data may be modified dynamically. Step by step, traverse the tree and ensure each node falls within valid value ranges. Hint: Use min and max constraints during recursion.

task_title: Find Lowest Common Ancestor in BST
task_description: This task requires finding the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two nodes in a BST. LCAs are used in hierarchical systems such as file directories and access control trees. Step by step, traverse the BST based on node values until the split point is found. Hint: Use BST ordering to optimize traversal.

task_title: Convert Sorted Array into Balanced BST
task_description: In this task, you will convert a sorted array into a height-balanced Binary Search Tree. Balanced trees improve search performance. Step by step, select middle element as root and recursively build left and right subtrees. Hint: Divide array into halves recursively.

This task introduces self-balancing trees by implementing AVL tree insertion. AVL trees automatically maintain height balance, which is critical in high-performance systems. Step by step, insert nodes and perform required rotations based on balance factors. Hint: Track height difference between subtrees.
expected_output: AVL tree remains balanced after insertion

task_description: In this task, you will implement left, right, left-right, and right-left rotations used in AVL trees. Understanding rotations is essential for self-balancing structures. Step by step, adjust pointers and heights during rotations. Hint: Test rotations independently before integration.

task_title: Check Balance Factor of AVL Tree
task_description: This task focuses on calculating and validating the balance factor of nodes in an AVL tree. Balance checking ensures tree remains height-balanced. Step by step, compute heights of subtrees and determine balance factor. Hint: Balance factor = height(left) - height(right).
evaluation_criteria: Accurate height calculation, correct formula usage

task_description: In this task, you will implement heap sort using a binary heap. Heap sort is an efficient comparison-based sorting algorithm used in system-level applications. Step by step, build a heap and repeatedly extract maximum or minimum element. Hint: Heapify array before sorting.
expected_output: Array sorted correctly using heap sort

task_description: This task requires implementing a priority queue using a heap data structure. Priority queues are used in scheduling, simulations, and networking. Step by step, implement enqueue with priority and dequeue highest priority element. Hint: Higher priority should be served first.

In this task, you will implement a graph using an adjacency list representation. Graphs model real-world networks such as social media and routing systems. Step by step, define vertex structure and store neighbors efficiently. Hint: Use lists for each vertex.

This task focuses on detecting cycles in an undirected graph. Cycle detection is essential in dependency resolution systems. Step by step, use DFS and track visited nodes and parent. Hint: Ignore parent node during DFS.
evaluation_criteria: Accurate DFS logic, correct parent handling

task_description: In this task, you will detect cycles in a directed graph using recursion stack or coloring method. Cycle detection is important in task scheduling systems. Step by step, track visited and recursion stack states. Hint: Back-edge indicates a cycle.
evaluation_criteria: Correct use of recursion stack, logical flow

task_description: This task requires implementing topological sorting for a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). Topological order is used in build systems and scheduling. Step by step, perform DFS and push nodes to stack after visiting neighbors. Hint: Only works for DAGs.

task_title: Implement Topological Sorting Using Kahn’s Algorithm
task_description: In this task, you will implement topological sorting using Kahn’s algorithm based on indegree counting. This approach is widely used in real systems. Step by step, calculate indegrees and process zero-indegree nodes. Hint: Use queue for processing.

This task focuses on finding the shortest path in an unweighted graph using BFS. Shortest path algorithms are used in routing and navigation systems. Step by step, maintain distance array and queue. Hint: BFS guarantees shortest path in unweighted graphs.

In this task, you will implement Dijkstra’s algorithm to find shortest paths in a weighted graph. This algorithm is widely used in GPS and network routing. Step by step, use a priority queue to select minimum distance node. Hint: Dijkstra does not support negative weights.

This task introduces the Bellman-Ford algorithm for graphs with negative weights. It is used in systems where edge costs can be negative. Step by step, relax edges repeatedly and check for negative cycles. Hint: Perform V-1 iterations.
expected_output: Correct shortest paths or negative cycle detected

task_description: In this task, you will implement Floyd-Warshall algorithm to compute all-pairs shortest paths. This algorithm is useful in dense graphs. Step by step, update distance matrix using dynamic programming. Hint: Triple nested loops required.

This task focuses on implementing the Disjoint Set data structure used in network connectivity and Kruskal’s algorithm. Step by step, implement find and union operations with path compression. Hint: Path compression improves efficiency.

In this task, you will use the Union-Find structure to detect cycles in an undirected graph. This approach is efficient for large graphs. Step by step, process edges and check root parents. Hint: Cycle exists if two vertices share same root.

task_title: Implement Minimum Spanning Tree Using Kruskal’s Algorithm
task_description: This task requires building a Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) using Kruskal’s algorithm. MSTs are used in network design and optimization. Step by step, sort edges and apply Union-Find to avoid cycles. Hint: Choose smallest edge first.

task_title: Implement Minimum Spanning Tree Using Prim’s Algorithm
task_description: In this task, you will implement Prim’s algorithm to find MST. Prim’s algorithm grows the tree incrementally. Step by step, use a priority queue to select minimum edge. Hint: Start from any vertex.
evaluation_criteria: Correct priority queue usage, accurate MST

task_description: This task focuses on implementing a Trie for efficient string storage and retrieval. Tries are used in autocomplete systems and dictionaries. Step by step, create nodes for characters and mark word endings. Hint: Each node represents a character.

In this task, you will implement word search functionality in a Trie. Searching efficiently is critical in text-processing systems. Step by step, traverse Trie nodes based on characters. Hint: Check end-of-word flag.

This task introduces hash tables using chaining to handle collisions. Hash tables are core components in caches and databases. Step by step, design hash function and linked list buckets. Hint: Good hash functions reduce collisions.

task_title: Implement Hash Table with Open Addressing
task_description: In this task, you will implement a hash table using open addressing. This approach resolves collisions by probing. Step by step, implement linear or quadratic probing. Hint: Handle table full condition.
expected_output: Hash table stores and retrieves correctly

task_description: This task focuses on comparing two hash collision resolution techniques. Step by step, analyze memory usage, performance, and complexity. Hint: Consider load factor impact.

task_title: Implement LRU Cache Using Data Structures
task_description: In this task, you will implement a Least Recently Used (LRU) cache using a combination of hash map and doubly linked list. LRU caches are used in operating systems and web browsers. Step by step, update usage order on access. Hint: Most recently used moves to front.

task_title: Design Real-World Problem Using Appropriate Data Structures
task_description: This task requires designing a solution to a real-world problem by selecting appropriate data structures. Step by step, analyze the problem, justify your choices, and implement a basic solution. Hint: Choose structures based on access and update needs.
expected_output: Problem solved using suitable data structures

task_title: Implement Segment Tree for Range Sum Queries
task_description: In this task, you will implement a Segment Tree to efficiently handle range sum queries on an array. Segment trees are widely used in competitive programming and real-world systems where frequent range queries and updates are required. Step by step, you must build the segment tree from an input array, implement a function to query the sum of elements within a given range, and optionally support point updates. Hint: Each node of the segment tree represents a range of the array.
expected_output: Segment tree correctly answers range sum queries
evaluation_criteria: Correct tree construction, accurate query results, efficient time complexity

task_title: Implement Segment Tree with Range Updates
task_description: This task extends the segment tree by supporting range updates along with queries. This is important for systems that require bulk updates efficiently. Step by step, enhance the segment tree to update values in a range and reflect changes in queries. Hint: Consider lazy propagation to optimize updates.
expected_output: Segment tree supports range updates correctly
evaluation_criteria: Correct lazy propagation logic, efficient updates and queries

task_description: In this task, you will implement a Fenwick Tree to perform prefix sum queries efficiently. Fenwick Trees are simpler alternatives to segment trees in many applications. Step by step, build the tree, implement update and query operations. Hint: Use index manipulation with bitwise operations.
evaluation_criteria: Correct index handling, accurate prefix sums

task_title: Compare Segment Tree and Fenwick Tree
task_description: This task requires comparing segment trees and Fenwick trees in terms of complexity, memory usage, and use cases. Step by step, analyze scenarios where each structure is preferable. Hint: Segment trees are more flexible, Fenwick trees are simpler.
evaluation_criteria: Depth of analysis, correctness of comparison

task_description: In this task, you will implement a graph using an adjacency matrix representation. This representation is useful for dense graphs. Step by step, create a matrix and populate edges based on input. Hint: Matrix size depends on number of vertices.
evaluation_criteria: Correct matrix initialization, accurate edge representation

task_description: This task focuses on finding strongly connected components in a directed graph using Kosaraju’s algorithm. SCCs are important in program analysis and network connectivity. Step by step, perform DFS, reverse the graph, and run DFS again. Hint: Order of traversal matters.
evaluation_criteria: Correct DFS order, accurate SCC detection

task_description: In this task, you will implement Tarjan’s algorithm to find strongly connected components in a graph using a single DFS traversal. Step by step, track discovery times and low-link values. Hint: Use a stack to track active nodes.

This task requires checking whether a graph is bipartite. Bipartite graphs are used in matching problems. Step by step, color vertices using BFS or DFS and ensure no two adjacent vertices share the same color. Hint: Two-coloring logic is key.

In this task, you will implement a graph coloring algorithm to minimize colors such that no adjacent vertices share the same color. This is used in scheduling and resource allocation problems. Step by step, assign colors based on constraints. Hint: Greedy approach is acceptable.

This task introduces backtracking by solving the N-Queens problem. Backtracking is essential for constraint satisfaction problems. Step by step, place queens row by row and backtrack when conflicts occur. Hint: Check columns and diagonals for safety.

In this task, you will implement a Sudoku solver using backtracking. This problem demonstrates recursion and constraint checking. Step by step, fill empty cells while maintaining Sudoku rules. Hint: Try numbers 1–9 and backtrack if invalid.

This task focuses on solving the Matrix Chain Multiplication problem using dynamic programming. This problem optimizes multiplication order to reduce operations. Step by step, compute cost matrix using DP. Hint: Optimal substructure is key.

In this task, you will implement the Longest Common Subsequence algorithm using dynamic programming. LCS is used in diff tools and DNA sequence analysis. Step by step, build DP table and reconstruct sequence. Hint: Compare characters and build table.

This task requires implementing the Longest Increasing Subsequence algorithm. LIS is used in sequence analysis and optimization problems. Step by step, use DP or optimized approach. Hint: Patience sorting improves performance.

In this task, you will solve the 0/1 Knapsack problem using dynamic programming. This problem models resource allocation scenarios. Step by step, build DP table based on weight and value. Hint: Choose max of including or excluding item.

This task focuses on solving the coin change problem using dynamic programming. Coin change is common in financial systems. Step by step, calculate minimum coins required. Hint: Initialize DP with large values.

In this task, you will implement edit distance (Levenshtein distance) algorithm. This is used in spell checkers and text matching. Step by step, compute DP table for insert, delete, replace. Hint: Choose minimum operation cost.

This task introduces string matching using hashing. Rabin-Karp is used in plagiarism detection systems. Step by step, compute rolling hash and compare substrings. Hint: Handle hash collisions carefully.

In this task, you will implement the Knuth-Morris-Pratt algorithm for efficient string matching. This avoids redundant comparisons. Step by step, compute LPS array and perform matching. Hint: Preprocessing pattern is essential.

This task requires designing a complete string search system using appropriate algorithms. Step by step, justify algorithm choice and implement core logic. Hint: Combine multiple string algorithms if needed.

In this task, you will implement the sliding window technique to solve array or string problems efficiently. Sliding window is widely used in performance-critical applications. Step by step, maintain window boundaries and update results dynamically. Hint: Expand and shrink window carefully.

This task focuses on solving problems using the two-pointer technique. This technique reduces time complexity in sorted data scenarios. Step by step, move pointers based on conditions. Hint: Start from both ends when appropriate.

In this task, you will design a solution using divide and conquer approach. This approach breaks problems into smaller subproblems. Step by step, divide input, solve recursively, and combine results. Hint: Merge sort follows this pattern.
This task focuses on implementing merge sort using divide and conquer. Merge sort is efficient and stable. Step by step, split array and merge sorted halves. Hint: Use auxiliary array for merging.
expected_output: Array sorted correctly using merge sort

task_description: In this task, you will implement quick sort algorithm. Quick sort is widely used due to its average efficiency. Step by step, select pivot and partition array. Hint: Choose pivot carefully to avoid worst case.
This task requires analyzing the worst-case time complexity of quick sort and how to avoid it. Step by step, explain scenarios and improvements. Hint: Randomized pivot helps.
In this task, you will implement randomized quick sort to reduce worst-case probability. Step by step, randomize pivot selection. Hint: Randomization improves average performance.

task_title: Implement Binary Search on Rotated Sorted Array
task_description: This task focuses on searching in a rotated sorted array. This problem improves binary search logic. Step by step, identify sorted half and narrow search. Hint: One half is always sorted.

task_title: Implement Median of Two Sorted Arrays
task_description: In this task, you will find the median of two sorted arrays efficiently. This problem tests advanced searching techniques. Step by step, use binary search partitioning. Hint: Divide arrays carefully.

task_title: Design Efficient Data Processing Pipeline Using Data Structures
task_description: This task requires designing a data processing pipeline using multiple data structures. Step by step, justify structure choices and implement key components. Hint: Combine queues, stacks, and trees as needed.
evaluation_criteria: Appropriate data structure usage, logical flow

task_description: In this task, you will implement an efficient representation of a sparse matrix where most elements are zero. Sparse matrices are used in scientific computing and memory-optimized systems. Step by step, store only non-zero elements using an appropriate data structure such as lists or maps, and implement display logic. Hint: Avoid storing zero values to save memory.
evaluation_criteria: Correct identification of non-zero elements, memory-efficient storage

task_description: This task focuses on adding two sparse matrices efficiently. This operation is important in scientific and engineering applications. Step by step, traverse stored non-zero elements and compute the sum without converting to dense matrices. Hint: Match row and column indices.

task_title: Implement Polynomial Representation Using Linked List
task_description: In this task, you will represent a polynomial using a linked list. Polynomial operations are common in symbolic computation. Step by step, store coefficients and powers in nodes and display polynomial expression. Hint: Keep terms ordered by power.

task_title: Perform Polynomial Addition Using Linked Lists
task_description: This task requires adding two polynomials represented as linked lists. Step by step, compare powers and sum coefficients. Hint: Traverse both lists simultaneously.

task_title: Implement Polynomial Multiplication Using Linked Lists
task_description: In this task, you will multiply two polynomials using linked lists. This task strengthens nested traversal logic. Step by step, multiply each term and combine like powers. Hint: Use temporary list for accumulation.

task_title: Implement Circular Linked List with Operations
task_description: This task focuses on implementing a circular linked list with insertion, deletion, and traversal. Circular lists are used in scheduling systems. Step by step, ensure last node points to head. Hint: Be careful with termination conditions.

task_title: Implement Josephus Problem Using Circular Linked List
task_description: In this task, you will solve the Josephus problem using a circular linked list. This problem tests traversal and deletion logic. Step by step, simulate elimination process. Hint: Count nodes carefully.

This task introduces skip lists, which provide fast search, insertion, and deletion. Skip lists are probabilistic alternatives to balanced trees. Step by step, implement multiple levels and random promotion. Hint: Randomization controls height.

In this task, you will implement a Bloom filter for fast membership testing. Bloom filters are used in caching and databases. Step by step, apply multiple hash functions and bit arrays. Hint: False positives are allowed.

task_title: Analyze False Positives in Bloom Filter
task_description: This task focuses on analyzing false positives in a Bloom filter. Step by step, explain why they occur and how parameters affect probability. Hint: More hash functions reduce false positives.

task_title: Implement Graph Shortest Path Using A* Algorithm
task_description: In this task, you will implement the A* search algorithm for finding shortest paths. A* is widely used in games and navigation systems. Step by step, combine cost and heuristic functions. Hint: Heuristic must be admissible.

task_title: Design Custom Heuristic for A* Algorithm
task_description: This task requires designing a heuristic function for A* algorithm. Step by step, justify heuristic choice and test correctness. Hint: Heuristic should not overestimate.

In this task, you will build an autocomplete system using a Trie. Autocomplete is used in search engines and editors. Step by step, insert words and return suggestions for prefixes. Hint: Traverse Trie to prefix node.
This task introduces suffix arrays for efficient string processing. Suffix arrays are used in text search systems. Step by step, generate suffixes and sort them. Hint: Store starting indices.

task_title: Implement Longest Repeated Substring Using Suffix Array
task_description: In this task, you will find the longest repeated substring using suffix array. This problem is used in data compression. Step by step, compare adjacent suffixes. Hint: Longest common prefix helps.
This task focuses on building a Cartesian Tree from an array. Cartesian trees are used in RMQ problems. Step by step, maintain heap property and inorder traversal. Hint: Use stack-based construction.

task_title: Implement Range Minimum Query Using Sparse Table
task_description: In this task, you will implement RMQ using sparse table technique. This is used for fast static range queries. Step by step, precompute intervals and answer queries in O(1). Hint: Preprocessing is key.

This task introduces heavy-light decomposition for trees. It is used in advanced tree queries. Step by step, decompose tree into heavy and light paths conceptually. Hint: Focus on idea, not full code.

In this task, you will study and explain Mo’s algorithm for offline range queries. This algorithm optimizes query ordering. Step by step, explain block decomposition. Hint: Query reordering reduces updates.

This task focuses on understanding persistent data structures that preserve previous versions. Used in versioned systems. Step by step, explain immutability and versioning. Hint: Functional structures help.

task_title: Implement Thread-Safe Stack Using Data Structures
task_description: In this task, you will design a thread-safe stack. Concurrency-safe structures are critical in systems programming. Step by step, ensure safe push/pop operations. Hint: Synchronization is required.
This task introduces lock-free data structure concepts. Lock-free designs improve concurrency performance. Step by step, explain atomic operations usage. Hint: CAS operations are key.
In this task, you will study bipartite graph matching. Matching algorithms are used in job allocation systems. Step by step, explain matching logic. Hint: Hungarian or Hopcroft-Karp.

task_title: Design Data Structure for Real-Time Analytics
task_description: This task focuses on designing data structures for real-time analytics systems. Step by step, choose structures for streaming data. Hint: Think queues and hash maps.
In this task, you will solve sliding window maximum using deque. This is used in signal processing. Step by step, maintain decreasing deque. Hint: Remove smaller elements.
This task introduces order statistics trees used for rank queries. Step by step, explain size augmentation. Hint: Store subtree sizes.
In this task, you will design a data structure optimized for minimal memory usage. Step by step, justify design decisions. Hint: Avoid redundancy.
This task requires designing a cache with custom eviction policy. Step by step, define eviction rules. Hint: FIFO, LFU, or hybrid.

task_title: Conduct Complexity Analysis of Advanced Data Structures
task_description: In this task, you will analyze time and space complexity of advanced data structures used so far. Step by step, compare operations. Hint: Focus on worst-case.

This final task represents a complete data structures capstone project. You must design, implement, document, and test a system using multiple data structures learned during the internship. This task simulates real-world expectations from a data structures intern. Hint: Treat this as a production-level project.
